stage.name: Cryptocurrency Guide
stage.description: Monetary theory, Bitcoin's UTXO model, elliptic curve cryptography, mining economics, and the economics of digital scarcity.

generatedAgentConfigs.name: Dr. Rivera
generatedAgentConfigs.persona: An economist who bridges classical monetary theory with cryptographic money systems.

elements.content: Money: From Commodity to Cryptography
Why Bitcoin is the hardest money ever created

21M
Hard cap

3.125
BTC/block

Properties of Sound Money

  • Scarce: Bitcoin hard cap = 21M coins — mathematically enforced
  • Durable: never degrades — digital information lasts forever
  • Portable: send $1B in seconds for <$0.01 in fees
  • Divisible: 1 BTC = 100,000,000 satoshis (0.00000001 BTC)
  • Fungible: each satoshi identical in protocol rules

Monetary Supply Comparison

  • M0 (physical cash): ~$6T globally. M1 (M0 + deposits): ~$50T
  • M2 (M1 + savings): ~$100T. Total global wealth: ~$500T
  • Bitcoin market cap: ~$1.7T (April 2026) — 0.3% of global wealth
  • USD M2 grew 40% in 2020-2021 → inflation followed
  • Bitcoin supply inflation: 0.83%/yr post-2024 halving

Halving & Scarcity Mechanics

  • Every 210,000 blocks (~4 years), block reward halves
  • 2009: 50 BTC/block. 2024: 3.125 BTC/block
  • ~93% of all Bitcoin already mined (April 2026)
  • Last Bitcoin mined: ~year 2140
  • Stock-to-Flow: SF = stock/annual_production. BTC SF ≈ 56 (gold: 60)
The UTXO Model
Unspent Transaction Outputs — Bitcoin's accounting ledger

85M
UTXO set

250B
Avg tx size

How UTXO Works

  • Every Bitcoin tx consumes UTXOs as inputs, creates new UTXOs as outputs
  • UTXOs must be spent entirely — change returned as new UTXO
  • Your 'balance' = sum of all UTXOs locked to your address
  • UTXO set size: ~85M entries (April 2026) — stored by full nodes
  • Coinbase tx: first tx in every block — creates new BTC from thin air

UTXO vs Account Model

  • UTXO (Bitcoin): parallel validation, privacy, no replay attacks
  • Account (Ethereum): simpler state, easier smart contract logic
  • UTXO: nonce not needed — each UTXO is unique by construction
  • Account: nonce required to prevent tx replay attacks
  • XRPL uses account model with sequence numbers

Transaction Fee Market

  • Fee = (tx_size_bytes) × (sat/byte_rate)
  • Mempool priority: higher sat/byte → confirmed first
  • Average tx size: ~250 bytes. SegWit tx: ~140 vBytes (cheaper)
  • SegWit (2017): fixes tx malleability, increases block capacity
  • Taproot (2021): Schnorr signatures, 20-30% fee reduction
Elliptic Curve Cryptography (secp256k1)
The math that proves ownership of Bitcoin without revealing your key

2¹²⁸
Attack ops

256-bit
Key size

The Curve & Key Generation

  • Curve equation: y² = x³ + 7 (mod p), p = 2²⁵⁶ − 2³² − 977
  • Generator point G: fixed public point on the curve
  • Private key: random 256-bit integer k ∈ [1, n-1]
  • Public key: K = k × G (elliptic curve point multiplication)
  • k × G is fast; inverting (finding k from K) is computationally infeasible

ECDLP & Security

  • Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP)
  • Best known attack: Pollard's rho — O(√n) ≈ 2¹²⁸ operations
  • 128-bit security level — equivalent to AES-128
  • Quantum threat: Shor's algorithm could break ECDLP in O(n³)
  • Bitcoin would need migration to post-quantum cryptography

Address Derivation

  • Public key (65 bytes) → SHA-256 → RIPEMD-160 → 20-byte hash
  • Add version byte → Base58Check → Bitcoin address
  • Address ≠ public key — extra privacy layer
  • P2PKH, P2SH, P2WPKH (SegWit), P2TR (Taproot) — 4 address types
  • Losing private key = losing funds permanently. ~4M BTC lost forever
Mining Economics
The business of creating new Bitcoin — profitability mechanics

$26M/day
BTC revenue

700 EH/s
Hash rate

Revenue Model

  • Block reward (2024+): 3.125 BTC/block × ~$60k = ~$187k/block
  • Transaction fees: 0.5–5% of block value (rises post-halving)
  • 144 blocks/day → ~$26M/day revenue across all miners
  • Break-even: Revenue > (Hash Rate × Power × Cost/kWh)
  • Hash price: $/TH/s/day — key profitability metric

Cost Structure

  • Electricity: largest cost — efficient miners use 0.024 J/GH
  • Top ASIC (Antminer S21): 200 TH/s at 3,500W = 17.5 J/TH
  • At $0.05/kWh: cost ≈ $0.021/TH/day → profitable above $40k BTC
  • Hardware capex: $10-30k per ASIC, 3-5 year lifespan
  • Data center, cooling, maintenance: 20-30% of total cost

Difficulty Adjustment

  • Adjusts every 2016 blocks to keep block time = 10 minutes
  • If blocks faster: difficulty ↑. Slower: difficulty ↓
  • Max adjustment per period: ±4× (prevents oscillation)
  • Hashrate: ~700 EH/s and growing — difficulty all-time high
  • Long-run: fee revenue must replace subsidy to secure chain
Altcoins, Ethereum & Token Standards
The ecosystem beyond Bitcoin

$3.1T
Total market

$180B+
Stablecoins

Market Landscape

  • Bitcoin dominance: ~55% of total crypto market cap (~$3.1T total)
  • Ethereum: ~17% dominance — programmable money layer
  • Altcoins: Solana, XRP, BNB, USDC — each solving different problems
  • Stablecoins: $180B+ in circulation — USDT, USDC, DAI
  • Crypto total market: ~$3.1T (April 2026)

ERC-20 Token Standard

  • ERC-20: 6 required functions (transfer, approve, allowance, etc.)
  • totalSupply(), balanceOf(), transfer(), approve(), transferFrom()
  • 100k+ ERC-20 tokens deployed — DeFi, governance, utility
  • ERC-721: NFTs — unique token IDs, non-fungible
  • ERC-1155: multi-token standard — fungible + NFT in one contract

Stablecoin Mechanisms

  • Fiat-backed (USDT, USDC): 1:1 USD reserves in bank
  • Crypto-backed (DAI): 150%+ collateralized — over-collateral
  • Algorithmic (UST): failed May 2022 — $40B wiped in 3 days
  • USDC: regulated, monthly attestations, redeemable 1:1
  • Central bank digital currencies (CBDCs): 130+ countries exploring
On-Chain Analysis — Reading the Blockchain
Metrics that reveal market psychology through raw blockchain data

MVRV
Key metric

SOPR
Profit ratio

MVRV & SOPR

  • MVRV: Market Value / Realized Value — expensive or cheap?
  • MVRV > 3.5: historically overvalued (sell signal). < 1: undervalued
  • SOPR: Spent Output Profit Ratio = price_sold / price_paid
  • SOPR > 1: average holder in profit. < 1: selling at loss
  • SOPR reset at 1 during bull markets = healthy support

Exchange Flows

  • Exchange inflow ↑: holders moving to sell (bearish)
  • Exchange outflow ↑: withdrawing to self-custody (bullish)
  • BTC exchange reserve: fell from 3.2M to 2.3M BTC (2020-2024)
  • Stablecoin supply on exchanges ↑: buying power building
  • Whale alert: wallets >1k BTC — track accumulation/distribution

NVT & Realized Cap

  • NVT ratio: Network Value / Transaction Volume — P/E for Bitcoin
  • High NVT: network overvalued relative to usage
  • Realized Cap: sum of each UTXO valued at last-moved price
  • Realized cap more stable than market cap — removes lost coins
  • MVRV = Market Cap / Realized Cap — tracks unrealized profit

content.html:
Bitcoin Halving & Supply Schedule

21,000,000
Max Supply (BTC)
19,700,000
Mined So Far
3.125 BTC
Current Block Reward
~2140
Last BTC Mined

Supply issued per era (% of 21M total):

Era 1 (2009-12)
50% — 10.5M BTC (50 BTC/block)
Era 2 (2012-16)
25% — 5.25M BTC (25 BTC/block)
Era 3 (2016-20)
12.5% — 2.625M BTC
Era 4 (2020-24)
6.25% — 1.3125M BTC
Era 5+ (2024+)
~6.25% remaining across all future eras

Each halving cuts new supply in half. 94% of all Bitcoin will be mined by ~2032. After 2140, miners earn fees only — the security budget must come entirely from transaction demand.

actions.text: This chart reveals something profound about Bitcoin's design: 94% of all Bitcoin that will ever exist has already been mined by 2032. The last Bitcoin won't be mined until 2140 — but the amounts become astronomically small. After that point, Bitcoin's security depends entirely on transaction fees.

questions.question: Alice wants to send 0.3 BTC to Bob. She has one UTXO worth 0.5 BTC. What outputs does the transaction create?

options.label: Two outputs: 0.3 BTC to Bob + ~0.2 BTC change back to Alice (minus fee)
The UTXO is partially spent — 0.2 BTC remains unspent on the same UTXO
Three outputs: 0.3 to Bob, 0.19 change, 0.01 fee to miner

questions.analysis: UTXOs must be fully consumed. A 0.5 BTC UTXO is entirely spent as an input. The transaction creates two outputs: 0.3 BTC to Bob and approximately 0.19998 BTC back to Alice. The difference (fee) is claimed implicitly by the miner — there is no explicit fee output.

questions.question: Bitcoin's private key is a random 256-bit integer. Approximately how many possible private keys exist?
questions.analysis: A 256-bit private key has 2²⁵⁶ possible values ≈ 1.158 × 10⁷⁷. For comparison, there are approximately 10⁸⁰ atoms in the observable universe. This makes brute-force search computationally impossible.

questions.question: The MVRV ratio is currently 0.85. What does this historically indicate?

options.label: Bitcoin is trading below its realized value — historical accumulation zone
Miners are unprofitable and will sell holdings

questions.analysis: MVRV below 1 means the current market price is below the average price at which all coins last moved (realized value). This has historically marked deep undervaluation and occurred only 4 times in Bitcoin's history — each preceded significant price appreciation.

questions.question: Which of the following stablecoin types are collateralized by real assets? (Select all that apply)
questions.analysis: USDC (cash/T-bills), USDT (commercial paper + cash), and DAI (crypto collateral at 150%+) are all asset-backed. UST was algorithmic — backed by LUNA token mint/burn mechanics with no real collateral, which caused its collapse.

questions.question: After the 2024 Bitcoin halving, the block subsidy is 3.125 BTC. What is the DAILY miner revenue at a BTC price of $70,000 (subsidy only, ignoring fees)?
questions.analysis: 144 blocks/day × 3.125 BTC/block = 450 BTC/day × $70,000 = $31,500,000/day in block subsidy alone. Adding transaction fees (~0.5 BTC/block) adds ~$5M more, for total ~$36.5M/day.

questions.question: Explain the Elliptic Curve Discrete Log Problem and why it makes Bitcoin's private key system secure. Include why the key derivation (K = k × G) is a one-way function.
questions.commentPrompt: Full marks for: (1) defining ECDLP — given K and G, find k is computationally infeasible, (2) point multiplication is easy forward (O(log k) doublings) but no known polynomial algorithm reverses it, (3) 256-bit key space makes brute force impossible, (4) distinction between classical vs quantum threat (Shor's algorithm).
